facts_disclosed: Owner-operator plan dead — insurance $14,000/yr, too risky

turns.content: yo so i need to vent about something because i been going back and forth on this for weeks and i finally made up my mind tonight and im sitting here at a loves in virginia at like 1am and i just feel weird about it
I'm here for it. What's going on? Sounds like you've been chewing on something heavy.

yeah so you know i been saving up to buy my own truck right like thats been the plan for a while now be an owner operator keep all the money be my own boss the whole thing well that dream is dead man its off the table completely
Wow, that's a big shift. What happened? I know that was something you were really working toward.

i finally sat down and did the math for real like actually did it not just the fantasy version where everything goes perfect and man the numbers dont work at all insurance alone is fourteen thousand dollars a year and thats just liability and cargo thats not even bobtail or physical damage you add all that up and its closer to eighteen twenty thousand a year just for insurance

That's a massive overhead before you even turn a wheel. Insurance is one of those costs that a lot of people underestimate when they're thinking about going independent. Did you look into getting quotes or was this from talking to other drivers?

both man i got actual quotes online and then i was at a truck stop outside of roanoke last week talking to this guy named dennis who went owner operator about two years ago and he was real honest with me he said after fuel and insurance and maintenance and his truck payment he is making less than me like less than what i make as a company driver at abf and he works more hours because he cant say no to any load

That's the part that never makes it into the dream version -- the reality of being unable to turn down bad loads because you've got fixed costs every month whether you're rolling or not. Dennis sounds like he gave you a straight-up honest picture. How much had you saved up so far?

i had about twelve thousand saved and a decent used truck is eighty five thousand so i wasnt even close yet but even if i was close the math still dont work because fuel is insane right now and maintenance is all on you and one bad month where the truck breaks down or you get sick or whatever and youre bankrupt like literally bankrupt and i was listening to this podcast about small business failure rates and the guy was saying 60 percent of owner operators fail in the first two years and that number just sat with me you know

That 60 percent number is sobering. And you're right that there's no safety net -- if your truck goes down for two weeks, you're still paying insurance and your loan but earning nothing. It's a huge gamble.

exactly and heres the thing if it was just me maybe i would take that gamble you know maybe i would roll the dice because thats who i am i been taking chances my whole life since my mom put us on that boat from port au prince but its not just me naomi is depending on me man i send money every month for her and if i go bankrupt chasing some dream what happens to her you know i cant do that to my daughter

That's a really mature way to look at it. The risk calculation changes completely when someone else is depending on you. Naomi needs stability more than you need to be your own boss. It's not giving up on ambition -- it's being smart about what matters most.

yeah and like abf is treating me good you know 74 cents a mile good benefits decent routes through the northeast so i can see naomi the peterbilt they gave me is basically new so why would i leave that to go stress about making truck payments and finding loads and dealing with brokers who try to lowball you every single time forget about it man the owner operator thing is dead its not happening

It sounds like you've done your homework and you're making a decision from a clear-headed place, not an emotional one. ABF is giving you solid pay, good equipment, and routes that let you see your daughter. That's not settling -- that's having your priorities straight.

i appreciate that because part of me feels like im giving up on something you know like every trucker talks about going owner operator like its the promised land and i was one of those guys and now im just going to be a company driver forever and thats okay i guess bondye bon at least i got a job i got my health mostly and naomi is good so whatever the dream is dead long live the new dream which is just being stable and present for my kid

There's nothing wrong with redefining the dream. A lot of people hold onto plans that don't work just because they announced them. You looked at the real numbers, talked to someone living it, and made a smart call. Being stable and present for Naomi is a better dream than being an owner-operator drowning in overhead. You should feel good about this, not weird.

yeah i think i just needed to say it out loud to make it real you know like putting it in words makes it final the twelve thousand i saved i might just keep it as emergency fund or maybe put some toward naomis future you know college or whatever she wants to do alright man its late im going to try to sleep in the cab sa k pase thats life right sometimes the smart move and the exciting move are not the same thing

That's a wise way to look at it. Putting that money toward an emergency fund or Naomi's future is a much better return than gambling it on a truck that might put you underwater. Get some rest and don't beat yourself up over this -- you made a grown-up decision. Take care.
